Axitinib versus sorafenib as first-line therapy in patients with metastatic renal-cell carcinoma: a randomised open-label phase 3 trial CrossMark
Thomas E Hutson, Vladimir Lesovoy, Salman Al-Shukri, Viktor P Stus, Oleg N Lipatov, Angel H Bair, Brad Rosbrook, Connie Chen, Sinil Kim, Nicholas J Vogelzang

Summary Background In previous clinical trials of patients with metastatic renal-cell carcinoma, patients treated with axitinib as second-line therapy had longer median progression-free survival than those treated with sorafenib. We therefore undertook a phase 3 trial comparing axitinib with sorafenib in patients with treatment-naive metastatic renal-cell carcinoma.

**Methods** In this randomised, open-label, phase 3 trial, patients with treatment-naive, measurable, clear-cell metastatic renal-cell carcinoma from 13 countries were stratified by Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status, and then randomly assigned (2:1) by a centralised registration system to receive axitinib 5 mg twice daily, or sorafenib 400 mg twice daily. The primary endpoint was progression-free survival, assessed by masked independent review committee in the intention-to-treat population. This ongoing trial is registered at ClinicalTrials.gov, NCT00920816.

Findings Between June 14, 2010, and April 21, 2011, we randomly assigned 192 patients to receive axitinib, and 96 patients to receive sorafenib. The cutoff date for this analysis was July 27, 2012, when 171 (59%) of 288 patients died or had disease progression, as assessed by the independent review committee. There was no significant difference in median progression-free survival between patients treated with axitinib or sorafenib (10.1 months [95% CI 7.2–12.1] vs 6.5 months [4.7–8.3], respectively; stratified hazard ratio 0.77, 95% CI 0.56–1.05). Any-grade adverse events that were more common (≥10% difference) with axitinib than with sorafenib were diarrhoea (94 [50%] of 189 patients vs 38 [40%] of 96 patients), hypertension (92 [49%] vs 28 [29%]), weight decrease (69 [37%] vs 23 [24%]), decreased appetite (54 [29%] vs 18 [19%]), dysphonia (44 [23%] vs ten [10%]), hypothyroidism (39 [21%] vs seven [7%]), and upper abdominal pain (31 [16%] vs six [6%]); those more common with sorafenib than with axitinib included palmar-plantar erythrodysaesthesia (PPE; 37 [39%] of 96 patients vs 50 [26%] of 189), rash (19 [20%] vs 18 [10%]), alopecia (18 [19%] vs eight [4%]), and erythema (18 [19%] vs five [3%]). The most common grade 3 or 4 adverse events in patients treated with axitinib included hypertension (26 [14%] of 189 patients), diarrhoea (17 [9%]), asthenia (16 [8%]), weight decrease (16 [8%]), and PPE (14 [7%]); common grade 3 or 4 adverse events in patients treated with sorafenib included PPE (15 [16%] of 96 patients), diarrhoea (five [5%]), and asthenia (five [5%]). Serious adverse events were reported in 64 (34%) of 189 patients receiving axitinib, and 24 (25%) of 96 patients receiving sorafenib.

Interpretation Axitinib did not significantly increase progression-free survival in patients with treatment-naive metastatic renal-cell carcinoma compared with those treated with sorafenib, but did demonstrate clinical activity and an acceptable safety profile.
Funding Pfizer Inc.

# Introduction The survival of patients with metastatic clear-cell renal-cell carcinoma has improved with the availability of drugs targeting VEGF, or its receptors (VEGFRs), and mTOR. Currently approved and available mTOR inhibitors include temsirolimus¹ and everolimus;² VEGF/VEGFR-targeted drugs are bevacizumab,³,⁴ sunitinib,⁵ sorafenib,⁶ pazopanib,⁷ and axitinib.⁸ Axitinib (Pfizer Inc, manufactured in Freiburg, Germany), a potent and selective second-generation VEGFR inhibitor,⁹ is approved in the USA,¹⁰ European Union, and elsewhere for treatment of advanced renal-cell carcinoma after failure of one previous systemic therapy. Previously, trials showed median

progression-free survival of 13.7 months with axitinib in cytokine-refractory metastatic renal-cell carcin- oma, 11,12 and median progression-free survival of 5.5 months and 5.7 months with sorafenib in cytokine- refractory and treatment-naive disease, 13 respectively. On the basis of these data, an ongoing randomised registrational trial (NCT00920816) of second-line axitinib versus sorafenib in Asian patients 14 was amended to add a separate global treatment-naive population that would be analysed independently from the second-line population. Sorafenib was chosen as the comparator because it was available in the regions where the trial was being done. Here, we report results from the treatment-naive population.

# Methods ## Study design and patients This phase 3 randomised, open-label trial evaluated efficacy, safety, and patient-reported outcomes for axitinib versus sorafenib in patients with treatment-naive metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. Patients for this trial were recruited from 13 countries (appendix). Key eligibility criteria included: aged 18 years or older; histologically or cytologically confirmed metastatic renal-cell carcinoma with a clear-cell component; unidimensionally measurable disease by Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors (RECIST, version 1.0); 15 Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status 0 or 1; at least 2 weeks since previous radiotherapy or surgery; adequate renal, hepatic, and haematological organ function; and life expectancy of at least 12 weeks. Antihypertensive therapy was allowed. Previous nephrectomy was not required. Previous systemic therapy for metastatic renal-cell carcinoma was not allowed; adjuvant interferon or interleukin, or both, were allowed if recurrence occurred more than 6 months after completion. Other exclusion factors included having uncontrolled hypertension (blood pressure higher than 140/90 mm Hg); brain metastases or CNS involvement; a history of other malignancy; or a significant medical history such as myocardial infarction, cerebrovascular accident, or transient ischaemic attack within the past 12 months.

The trial was undertaken in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki, the International Conference on Harmonisation guidelines on Good Clinical Practice, and applicable local regulatory requirements and laws. All patients provided written informed consent. The protocol, amendments, and informed consent forms were approved by an institutional review board or independent ethics committee at each study centre.

**Randomisation and masking** Patients were stratified according to ECOG performance status (0 or 1), and were randomly assigned in a 2:1 ratio by a centralised registration system to receive either axitinib 5 mg twice daily, or sorafenib 400 mg twice daily. Randomisation sequences, used by all centres, were generated by the central randomisation administrator using permuted blocks with a block size of six (four for axitinib to two for sorafenib) within each performance status stratum. Treatment group assignment for an individual patient was obtained at the time of enrolment by the investigating site via web interface in a manner that concealed all past and future assignments. Patients and investigators were unmasked to treatment. Radiologists participating in independent review committee assessment of the primary endpoint were masked to treatment.
## Procedures Axitinib was given orally at a starting dose of 5 mg twice daily with food, and sorafenib was given orally at a starting dose of 400 mg twice daily without food, both in

4-week cycles. Axitinib doses could be increased first to 7 mg twice daily, and subsequently to 10 mg twice daily, for patients who had not had any grade two or worse treatment-related adverse events (as defined by Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events, or CTCAE, version 3.0) for at least 2 weeks and had blood pressure 150/90 mm Hg or lower. On the basis of adverse events, or laboratory abnormalities, axitinib dose could be reduced to 3 mg twice daily, and then 2 mg twice daily, and sorafenib dose could be reduced to 400 mg once daily, and then 400 mg every other day. Both drugs were given according to their approved labelling, such that dose increases were allowed with axitinib,¹º but not with sorafenib.¹⁶ Patients who had disease progression but who had clinical benefit from axitinib or sorafenib were eligible for continued treatment. Crossover between the two drugs was not allowed during the study.

Baseline tumour assessments included CT or MRI scans of brain, chest, abdomen, and pelvis, and a bone scan. CT or MRI (not including brain) and bone scans (only if baseline lesions were present) were repeated at week 6, week 12, every 8 weeks thereafter, and end of study treatment. All CT, MRI, and bone scans were centrally reviewed by an independent review committee.

Physical examinations were done at baseline, day 1 (if day 1 was more than 7 days since baseline), week 2, week 4, every 4 weeks thereafter, and end of study treatment. Blood pressure was monitored at each clinic visit, and twice a day at home by patients. Assessment of ECOG performance status and laboratory tests for haematology, blood chemistry, and urinalysis occurred at baseline, day 1, every 4 weeks, and end of study treatment. Thyroid function tests (free tri-iodothyronine [T3], free thyroxine [T4], and thyroid-stimulating hormone [TSH]) were done at baseline. TSH was then assessed at weeks 2, 4, 8, and 12, and every 8 weeks thereafter. Free T3/T4 was assessed as clinically indicated. Safety was assessed throughout the study, with adverse events graded according to CTCAE.

The primary endpoint was progression-free survival— time from randomisation to objective tumour progression or death—assessed by independent review committee. Secondary endpoints were assessing the proportion of patients who achieved an objective response, response duration, overall survival, safety, and patient-reported outcomes. Objective response rate (assessed by independent review committee) was defined as the proportion of patients with confirmed (persistence on repeat imaging after at least 4 weeks) complete or partial response as per RECIST criteria. 15

Patient-reported outcomes were assessed on day 1 of treatment, every 4 weeks during treatment, at the end of study treatment, and 28 days after last dose of study treatment. Assessments used the Functional Assessment of Cancer Therapy–Kidney Symptom Index (FKSI)-15,¹⁷ FKSI-Disease Related Symptoms (FKSI-DRS) subscale,¹⁸ and European Quality of Life-5 Dimensions (EQ-5D) questionnaires.¹⁹ FKSI-15 consists of 15 items; FKSI-DRS

consists of nine of the 15 items that focus on kidney cancer-specific symptoms. Higher scores reflected a better health state. The EQ-5D is a brief, general health- status questionnaire consisting of five questions on current health state and one visual analogue scale summary item on overall health status.

Statistical analysis The trial tested the null hypothesis that median progression-free survival with axitinib was 5.5 months versus the alternative hypothesis that it was at least 9.8 months (78% improvement), corresponding to a hazard ratio (HR) of 0.56 (overall one-sided a=0.025). 148 progression-free survival events were needed for a log-rank test with an overall one-sided significance level of 0.025 to have 90% power. An estimated total of 247 patients were to be enrolled.

Efficacy and patient-reported outcomes were analysed in the intention-to-treat (ITT) population (all treatment- naive patients randomly assigned to groups); safety was analysed in patients who received at least one dose of study drug. A third-party independent data monitoring committee monitored safety on a periodic basis as well as interim efficacy. At the interim futility analysis, planned when 74 patients had investigator-assessed disease progression or death (half of those required for final analysis), the data monitoring committee could recommend early trial stopping, or increase the number of patients required to be observed with disease progression or death for final analysis by up to 48, based on conditional power (≥73% and <90%).

The Kaplan-Meier method was used to estimate median progression-free survival; two-sided 95% CIs were calculated. Progression-free survival was compared with a stratified, one-sided, log-rank test. Cox proportional hazard model explored the potential influence of ECOG performance status and patient characteristics (ie, age, sex, ethnic origin, and Memorial Sloan-Kettering Cancer Center [MSKCC] risk group) on progression-free survival. Estimated HR and two-sided 95% CI were provided. A test of the proportional hazards assumption concluded that there was no violation of that assumption. Additionally, median progression-free survival, HRs, and two-sided 95% CIs were provided for each level of ECOG performance status and baseline characteristics.

The Cochran-Mantel-Haenszel test was used for stratified analyses comparing the proportion of patients achieving an objective response between treatments. The Kaplan-Meier method was used to estimate median duration of response; two-sided 95% CIs were calculated. Final analysis of overall survival requires 178 deaths; these data are currently immature. Patients are being followed up for survival every 3 months after study treatment discontinuation.
Adverse events and laboratory abnormalities were reported. Mean (SD) and median FKSI-15, FKSI-DRS,

EQ-5D scores and changes from baseline were reported. A repeated-measures mixed-effects model compared patient-reported outcomes between treatments; variables included treatment, time, and treatment-by-time, with baseline as covariate. Minimally important differences were prespecified as five or more points for FKSI-15 and three or more points for FKSI-DRS, based on published reports. 17,18 Survival analysis methods evaluated time-to- deterioration composite endpoints, where time-to- deterioration was a composite definition consisting of time to death, tumour progression, or meaningful worsening in quality of life (defined by minimally important difference), whichever occurred first. SAS version 9.2 was used for all statistical analyses. The study is registered at ClinicalTrials.gov, NCT00920816.

## Results Between June 14, 2010, and April 21, 2011, 288 patients with treatment-naive metastatic renal-cell carcinoma were enrolled. The highest enrolment occurred in Ukraine (n=61), Russia (n=58), and India (n=34); with enrolment occurring in ten other countries (appendix). Patient demographic and baseline characteristics were balanced between treatment groups (table 1). 192 patients were randomly assigned to axitinib and 96 to sorafenib (figure 1). Three patients assigned to axitinib did not receive treatment and were not included in the safety population.

The interim analysis occurred after 80 (54%) of the required 148 patients died or had disease progression as per investigator assessment, at which time the data monitoring committee recommended increasing the required number of patients with disease progression, per independent review committee assessment, or death to 169. The cutoff date for the analyses reported here was July 27, 2012, when 171 (59%) of 288 patients died or had disease progression, as assessed by the independent review committee. As of that date, 125 (65%) of 192 and 69 (72%) of 96 patients had discontinued axitinib and sorafenib, respectively (figure 1). The most common

reasons for treatment discontinuation were disease progression and death. Data are not available for median follow-up.

The primary endpoint of median progression-free survival, as assessed by independent review committee, was 10.1 months (95% CI 7.2–12.1) with axitinib and 6.5 months (4.7–8.3) with sorafenib (figure 2A). This difference was not significant (stratified HR 0.77, 95% CI 0.56–1.05; one-sided p=0.038). In a prespecified analysis, median progression-free survival was longer with axitinib versus sorafenib in patients with ECOG performance status 0 (figure 2B), but not in patients with ECOG performance status 1 (figure 2C). There were no significant differences in other prespecified analyses (figure 3). In a post-hoc analysis of 250 patients with previous nephrectomy, median progression-free survival as assessed by independent review committee was also improved with axitinib versus sorafenib (figure 2D).

More patients had an objective response, as assessed by independent review committee, with axitinib than with sorafenib (62 [32%] of 192 vs 14 [15%] of 96; risk ratio 2.21, 95% CI 1.31-3.75, stratified one-sided p=0.0006; table 2). No patient had a complete response with either treatment. Median duration of response was 14.7 months (95% CI 11.0-not estimable [NE]) with axitinib and 14.3 months (11.3-NE) with sorafenib. Median treatment duration was longer (373 days [range 4-743] vs 303.5 days [range 6-646]) and median relative dose intensity (total dose administered / total dose assigned×100) was greater (125% vs 98%) with axitinib than with sorafenib. The axitinib dose was increased above 10 mg daily in 134 (71%) of 189 patients; 29 (15%) patients received 12-14 mg daily (median 28 days [range 2-498]), and 105 (56%) patients received 20 mg daily (median 168 days [range 5-640]). A lower proportion of patients treated with axitinib had dose reductions (47 [25%] of 189 vs 41 [43%] of 96), or interruptions (136 [72%] of 189 vs 75 [78%] of 96) than those treated with sorafenib. Adverse events led to dose interruptions in 94 (50%) of 189 patients receiving axitinib and 44 (46%) of 96 receiving sorafenib. Five (3%) of 189 patients discontinued axitinib and two (2%) of 96 discontinued sorafenib because of a treatment-related adverse event.

The most frequent all-grade, all-causality adverse events were diarrhoea, hypertension, weight decrease, and fatigue with axitinib versus diarrhoea, palmar- plantar erythrodysaesthesia (PPE), and hypertension with sorafenib (table 3). Adverse events more common (10% or greater difference) with axitinib versus sorafenib were diarrhoea, hypertension, weight decrease, decreased appetite, dysphonia, and hypothyroidism (table 3), and upper abdominal pain (31 [16%] of 189 vs six [6%] of 96 patients); those more common with sorafenib versus axitinib included PPE and rash (table 3), alopecia (18 [19%] of 96 vs eight [4%] of 189), and erythema (18 [19%] of 96 vs five [3%] of 189). The most frequently reported grade 3 or 4 adverse events were hypertension,

diarrhoea, weight decrease, and asthenia in axitinib-treated patients and PPE, diarrhoea, and asthenia in sorafenib-treated patients (table 3). Grade 3 or worse all-causality adverse events more common (5% or greater difference) in patients treated with axitinib were hypertension and weight decrease; PPE was more common in those treated with sorafenib. All-causality serious adverse events were reported in 64 (34%) of 189 patients receiving axitinib and 24 (25%) of 96 patients receiving sorafenib, including ten (5%) and seven (7%) patients, respectively, with treatment-related serious adverse events. The most common all-causality serious adverse events excluding disease progression were diarrhoea (five [3%]), pleural effusion (three [2%]), and cardiac arrest (three [2%]) in the 189 patients in the axitinib group and abdominal pain (three [3%]), and pleural effusion (three [3%]) in the 96 patients in the sorafenib group. Serious treatment-related adverse events in the 189 axitinib-treated patients included diarrhoea (four [2%] patients) and atrial flutter, cardiac arrest, myocardial infarction, gastric ulcer, gastrointestinal haemorrhage, melaena, nausea, rectal haemorrhage, vomiting, retroperitoneal haematoma, asthenia, mucosal inflammation, dehydration, decreased appetite, aortoduodenal fistula, and hypertensive crisis (one [<1%] patient each). Serious treatment-related adverse events in the 96 sorafenib-treated patients were acute myocardial infarction, abdominal pain, diarrhoea, nausea, impaired healing, bacterial skin infection, pleural effusion, erythema multiforme, and PPE (one [1%] patient each).

erythema multiforme, and PPE (one [1%] patient each). At data cutoff, 78 (41%) of 192 patients in the axitinib group and 36 (38%) of 96 in the sorafenib group had died. The most common cause of death was disease progression (67 [86%] patients in the axitinib group and 35 [97%] in the sorafenib group). One patient in the axitinib group died of treatment-related cardiac arrest; no patients in the sorafenib group had a treatment-related death. Additional causes of death were either unknown (six [8%] in the axitinib group) or other (four [5%] in the axitinib group and one [3%] in the sorafenib group).

Eight patients from one site were excluded from analyses of patient-reported outcomes because of issues with patient signature verification when they completed the question- naires. However, at the end of treatment when most patients discontinued because of progression, mean FKSI-15 (39.1 [SD 9.1] for axitinib and 39.5 [SD 8.9] for sorafenib) and FKSI-DRS (26.6 [SD 5.5] for axitinib and 26.8 [SD 6.0] for sorafenib) scores worsened in both groups. No significant differences were noted between axitinib and sorafenib in median time-to-deterioration composite endpoints, using FKSI-15 (5·3 months [95% CI 4·5-6·5] _vs_ 4·7 months [4·4-6·5]; unstratified HR 1·00, 95% CI 0·76-1·32; one-sided p=0·51) or FKSI-DRS (6·3 months [95% CI 4·5-6·5] _vs_ 4·7 months [4·4-6·5]; unstratified HR 0·98; 95% CI 0·74-1·29; one-sided p=0·43). For EQ-5D, mean baseline (0·71 [SD 0·25] _vs_ 0·71 [0·27]) and end-of-treatment (0·64 [SD 0·27] _vs_ 0·59 [0·29]) scores were similar with axitinib versus sorafenib. Repeated-measures mixed-effects analyses revealed no significant difference between the two treatments in overall scores with respect to FKSI-15, FKSI-DRS, or EQ-5D (data not shown).

## Discussion In this randomised phase 3 trial comparing axitinib versus sorafenib as first-line therapy, although median progression-free survival was numerically longer with axitinib than with sorafenib, the difference was not significant. The trial was designed with the prediction of an ambitious 4·3-month (78%) improvement in median progression-free survival over the active comparator sorafenib. Sorafenib showed longer median progression- free survival than expected, ⁶,¹³ and axitinib showed shorter median progression-free survival than previously observed in patients with cytokine-refractory metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. ¹¹,¹²

In a prespecified analysis, we observed a 7-1-month difference in median progression-free survival with axitinib versus sorafenib in patients with ECOG perfor- mance status 0, but no difference in patients with per- formance status 1. To verify that patients with ECOG performance status 1 were accurately classified, and met entry criteria, baseline ECOG performance status was audited at five of the highest enrolling sites; no obvious evidence of ineligibility based on this measure was noted from patients' medical records.

A similar trend in ECOG performance status subgroup analysis was observed in the TIVO-1 study. 21 Patients with ECOG performance status 0 treated with tivozanib had significantly longer median progression-free survival compared to those treated with sorafenib (14.8 months vs 9.1 months, respectively; two-sided p=0.004); however, no difference was observed between the two treatment groups in patients with ECOG performance status 1 (9.1 months vs 9.0 months, respectively; two-sided p=0.59). Median progression-free survival with sorafenib was similar in patients with ECOG performance status 0 and 1 in both TIVO-121 (9.1 months for patients with performance status 0 and 9.0 months for those with performance status 1) and the present trial (6.6 months

for patients with performance status 0 and 6·4 months for those with performance status 1). It is intriguing that ECOG performance status seemed to affect progression- free survival in patients receiving axitinib or tivozanib, but not in those receiving sorafenib. This pattern was not noted in the global phase 3 AXIS trial⁸ that compared second-line axitinib versus sorafenib in patients with metastatic renal-cell carcinoma.

A large proportion of patients in AXIS were recruited from North America and western Europe. By contrast, few patients in the current trial were recruited from those areas. Instead, more than half of the patients in this trial were enrolled from eastern Europe, where resource limitations and local practice standards might have affected the type of patient enrolled, or patient management. Patients from lower socioeconomic regions might have had increased disease burdens due to delays in both diagnosis and treatment, thus possibly worsening progression-free survival outcomes, especially in patients with ECOG performance status 1. Another hypothesis might be that eastern European patients who have access to clinical trials might have a higher socioeconomic status, thus leading to increased compliance and better than expected outcomes in patients with ECOG performance status 0. However it is worth noting that ECOG performance status 0 and 1 are broad categories that allow much clinical latitude and remain somewhat subjective. As improved clinical²² and molecular²³ prognostic categories are developed, older classifications (eg, ECOG performance status) are likely to be replaced with more objective ones in future clinical trials.

Although caution should be exercised when comparing across trials, the AXIS⁸ trial appeared to include more patients with MSKCC poor risk than our trial; however, this difference might be explained by use of two different MSKCC risk criteria, based on line of therapy, in the present trial²⁰ versus the AXIS trial.²⁴ More patients in the present trial had axitinib dose increases, and fewer had axitinib dose reductions, compared with patients in the AXIS trial.⁸ This difference might be due to patients receiving first-line versus second-line therapy being able to better tolerate dose increases, and to differences between one of the axitinib dose-escalation criteria (in the AXIS trial, dose increases were not allowed in patients receiving antihypertensive therapy, whereas they were allowed here).

This trial has shown that axitinib has clinical activity in the first-line treatment of metastatic renal-cell carcinoma (panel). At the cutoff date for our analysis, only 114 (40%) of 288 patients had died, and median overall survival had not been reached. We continue to follow patients for overall survival in the study, and plan to report the definitive results when they are mature and more interpretable. The safety profiles for axitinib and sorafenib in the present study were in line with adverse events reported in previously treated patients in AXIS, 8 and those reported with other VEGFR inhibitors in

"**Panel: Research in context** **Systematic review** A systematic literature review was not done as part of the planning for this trial. However, clinical trial data had shown median progression-free survival of 13·7 months with axitinib in cytokine-refractory metastatic renal-cell carcinoma11,12 and median progression-free survival of 5·5 months6 and 5·7 months13 with sorafenib in cytokine-refractory and treatment-naive disease, respectively. On the basis of these results, we amended a randomised trial of second-line axitinib versus sorafenib in Asian patients with advanced renal-cell carcinoma to include a separate global treatment-naive population."

**Interpretation** To our knowledge, this is the first phase 3 trial evaluating axitinib, a VEGF-receptor inhibitor, in patients with treatment-naive metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. Axitinib was found to have clinical activity, and a manageable safety profile in these patients. Median progression-free survival with axitinib in the overall population is similar to that reported in other first-line studies evaluating sunitinib⁵ or pazopanib⁷ for the treatment of metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. Given its clinical activity and differentiated toxicity profile, axitinib is a welcome addition to the treatment armamentarium for patients with metastatic renal-cell carcinoma.
treatment-naive patients.^(5,7,13,21,25) Adverse event profiles differed between agents.

Baseline mean FKSI-15 and FKSI-DRS scores in the present trial were similar to those observed in a general US population,26 and so improvement in quality of life was not expected. Maintenance of quality of life was regarded as a good outcome. A consistent finding in observed mean FKSI-15 and FKSI-DRS scores was shown here as in the AXIS trial,27 in which quality of life worsened at the end of treatment with both axitinib and sorafenib, presumably because disease progression worsened quality of life. In the longitudinal analysis, postbaseline scores for patient-reported outcomes were similar between axitinib and sorafenib over time, consistent with results from AXIS.27 Unlike the AXIS trial,8 although the time-to-deterioration composite endpoints of time to death, tumour progression, or meaningful worsening in quality of life appeared longer for axitinib versus sorafenib, the differences were not significant; thus for both patient groups clinical efficacy (progression-free survival) was achieved without sacrificing quality of life.

This study had several limitations. The sample size was relatively small, designed to detect a large magnitude of difference. Sorafenib, the comparator in this trial, was chosen because of its activity and broad indication in advanced renal-cell carcinoma,¹⁶ as well as its availability in the regions where the trial was undertaken and experience with its use in the AXIS trial.⁸ In retrospect, this choice might have been a limitation of the present study in that axitinib was not compared with what are regarded as standard first-line drugs, sunitinib or pazopanib.²⁵ Additionally, patients and investigators were unmasked to treatment, which might have led to bias in safety and assessment of patient-reported outcomes; efficacy endpoints, however, were assessed by masked
www.thelancet.com/oncology Vol 14 December 2013

independent review committee. Selection bias due to use of permuted blocks for generation of randomisation lists is another potential limitation; however, the effect is thought to be minimal since the investigators did not have access to the central randomisation list (used for all centres) and, hence, would not have been able to predict treatment assignment.

To our knowledge, this is the first randomised phase 3 trial comparing axitinib with an active agent in the first-line setting. Although there was no significant difference between patient groups' progression-free survival, axitinib showed clinical activity and an acceptable safety profile as first-line therapy for patients with treatment-naive metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. Given that no therapy is curative for patients with this disease, continuing research is needed to identify new active drugs.

Articles Axitinib versus sorafenib as first-line therapy in patients with metastatic renal-cell carcinoma: a randomised open-label phase 3 trial CrossMark Thomas E Hutson, Vladimir Lesovoy, Salman Al-Shukri, Viktor P Stus, Oleg N Lipatov, Angel H Bair, Brad Rosbrook, Connie Chen, Sinil Kim, Nicholas J Vogelzang Summary Background In previous clinical trials of patients with metastatic renal-cell carcinoma, patients treated with axitinib as second-line therapy had longer median progression-free survival than those treated with sorafenib. We therefore undertook a phase 3 trial comparing axitinib with sorafenib in patients with treatment-naive metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. **Methods** In this randomised, open-label, phase 3 trial, patients with treatment-naive, measurable, clear-cell metastatic renal-cell carcinoma from 13 countries were stratified by Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status, and then randomly assigned (2:1) by a centralised registration system to receive axitinib 5 mg twice daily, or sorafenib 400 mg twice daily. The primary endpoint was progression-free survival, assessed by masked independent review committee in the intention-to-treat population. This ongoing trial is registered at ClinicalTrials.gov, NCT00920816. Findings Between June 14, 2010, and April 21, 2011, we randomly assigned 192 patients to receive axitinib, and 96 patients to receive sorafenib. The cutoff date for this analysis was July 27, 2012, when 171 (59%) of 288 patients died or had disease progression, as assessed by the independent review committee. There was no significant difference in median progression-free survival between patients treated with axitinib or sorafenib (10.1 months [95% CI 7.2–12.1] vs 6.5 months [4.7–8.3], respectively; stratified hazard ratio 0.77, 95% CI 0.56–1.05). Any-grade adverse events that were more common (≥10% difference) with axitinib than with sorafenib were diarrhoea (94 [50%] of 189 patients vs 38 [40%] of 96 patients), hypertension (92 [49%] vs 28 [29%]), weight decrease (69 [37%] vs 23 [24%]), decreased appetite (54 [29%] vs 18 [19%]), dysphonia (44 [23%] vs ten [10%]), hypothyroidism (39 [21%] vs seven [7%]), and upper abdominal pain (31 [16%] vs six [6%]); those more common with sorafenib than with axitinib included palmar-plantar erythrodysaesthesia (PPE; 37 [39%] of 96 patients vs 50 [26%] of 189), rash (19 [20%] vs 18 [10%]), alopecia (18 [19%] vs eight [4%]), and erythema (18 [19%] vs five [3%]). The most common grade 3 or 4 adverse events in patients treated with axitinib included hypertension (26 [14%] of 189 patients), diarrhoea (17 [9%]), asthenia (16 [8%]), weight decrease (16 [8%]), and PPE (14 [7%]); common grade 3 or 4 adverse events in patients treated with sorafenib included PPE (15 [16%] of 96 patients), diarrhoea (five [5%]), and asthenia (five [5%]). Serious adverse events were reported in 64 (34%) of 189 patients receiving axitinib, and 24 (25%) of 96 patients receiving sorafenib. Interpretation Axitinib did not significantly increase progression-free survival in patients with treatment-naive metastatic renal-cell carcinoma compared with those treated with sorafenib, but did demonstrate clinical activity and an acceptable safety profile. Funding Pfizer Inc. # Introduction The survival of patients with metastatic clear-cell renal-cell carcinoma has improved with the availability of drugs targeting VEGF, or its receptors (VEGFRs), and mTOR. Currently approved and available mTOR inhibitors include temsirolimus¹ and everolimus;² VEGF/VEGFR-targeted drugs are bevacizumab,³,⁴ sunitinib,⁵ sorafenib,⁶ pazopanib,⁷ and axitinib.⁸ Axitinib (Pfizer Inc, manufactured in Freiburg, Germany), a potent and selective second-generation VEGFR inhibitor,⁹ is approved in the USA,¹⁰ European Union, and elsewhere for treatment of advanced renal-cell carcinoma after failure of one previous systemic therapy. Previously, trials showed median progression-free survival of 13.7 months with axitinib in cytokine-refractory metastatic renal-cell carcin- oma, 11,12 and median progression-free survival of 5.5 months and 5.7 months with sorafenib in cytokine- refractory and treatment-naive disease, 13 respectively. On the basis of these data, an ongoing randomised registrational trial (NCT00920816) of second-line axitinib versus sorafenib in Asian patients 14 was amended to add a separate global treatment-naive population that would be analysed independently from the second-line population. Sorafenib was chosen as the comparator because it was available in the regions where the trial was being done. Here, we report results from the treatment-naive population. I.I. Mechnikov", Dnipropetrovs'k, Ukraine (Prof V P Stus MD); Republican Clinical Oncology Dispensary, Ufa, Russia (Prof O N Lipatov MD); Pfizer Oncology, San Diego, CA, USA (A H Bair PhD, B Rosbrook MS, S Kim MD); Pfizer Oncology, New York, NY, USA (C Chen PharmD); and Comprehensive Cancer Centers of Nevada, Las Vegas, NV, USA, and US Oncology Research, Houston, TX, USA (Prof N J Vogelzang MD) Correspondence to: Prof Thomas E Hutson, Baylor Sammons Cancer Center, Dallas, TX 75246, USA thomas.hutson@usoncology. com www.thelancet.com/oncology Vol 14 December 2013 1287 Articles See Online for appendix # Methods ## Study design and patients This phase 3 randomised, open-label trial evaluated efficacy, safety, and patient-reported outcomes for axitinib versus sorafenib in patients with treatment-naive metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. Patients for this trial were recruited from 13 countries (appendix). Key eligibility criteria included: aged 18 years or older; histologically or cytologically confirmed metastatic renal-cell carcinoma with a clear-cell component; unidimensionally measurable disease by Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors (RECIST, version 1.0); 15 Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status 0 or 1; at least 2 weeks since previous radiotherapy or surgery; adequate renal, hepatic, and haematological organ function; and life expectancy of at least 12 weeks. Antihypertensive therapy was allowed. Previous nephrectomy was not required. Previous systemic therapy for metastatic renal-cell carcinoma was not allowed; adjuvant interferon or interleukin, or both, were allowed if recurrence occurred more than 6 months after completion. Other exclusion factors included having uncontrolled hypertension (blood pressure higher than 140/90 mm Hg); brain metastases or CNS involvement; a history of other malignancy; or a significant medical history such as myocardial infarction, cerebrovascular accident, or transient ischaemic attack within the past 12 months. The trial was undertaken in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki, the International Conference on Harmonisation guidelines on Good Clinical Practice, and applicable local regulatory requirements and laws. All patients provided written informed consent. The protocol, amendments, and informed consent forms were approved by an institutional review board or independent ethics committee at each study centre. **Randomisation and masking** Patients were stratified according to ECOG performance status (0 or 1), and were randomly assigned in a 2:1 ratio by a centralised registration system to receive either axitinib 5 mg twice daily, or sorafenib 400 mg twice daily. Randomisation sequences, used by all centres, were generated by the central randomisation administrator using permuted blocks with a block size of six (four for axitinib to two for sorafenib) within each performance status stratum. Treatment group assignment for an individual patient was obtained at the time of enrolment by the investigating site via web interface in a manner that concealed all past and future assignments. Patients and investigators were unmasked to treatment. Radiologists participating in independent review committee assessment of the primary endpoint were masked to treatment. ## Procedures Axitinib was given orally at a starting dose of 5 mg twice daily with food, and sorafenib was given orally at a starting dose of 400 mg twice daily without food, both in 4-week cycles. Axitinib doses could be increased first to 7 mg twice daily, and subsequently to 10 mg twice daily, for patients who had not had any grade two or worse treatment-related adverse events (as defined by Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events, or CTCAE, version 3.0) for at least 2 weeks and had blood pressure 150/90 mm Hg or lower. On the basis of adverse events, or laboratory abnormalities, axitinib dose could be reduced to 3 mg twice daily, and then 2 mg twice daily, and sorafenib dose could be reduced to 400 mg once daily, and then 400 mg every other day. Both drugs were given according to their approved labelling, such that dose increases were allowed with axitinib,¹º but not with sorafenib.¹⁶ Patients who had disease progression but who had clinical benefit from axitinib or sorafenib were eligible for continued treatment. Crossover between the two drugs was not allowed during the study. Baseline tumour assessments included CT or MRI scans of brain, chest, abdomen, and pelvis, and a bone scan. CT or MRI (not including brain) and bone scans (only if baseline lesions were present) were repeated at week 6, week 12, every 8 weeks thereafter, and end of study treatment. All CT, MRI, and bone scans were centrally reviewed by an independent review committee. Physical examinations were done at baseline, day 1 (if day 1 was more than 7 days since baseline), week 2, week 4, every 4 weeks thereafter, and end of study treatment. Blood pressure was monitored at each clinic visit, and twice a day at home by patients. Assessment of ECOG performance status and laboratory tests for haematology, blood chemistry, and urinalysis occurred at baseline, day 1, every 4 weeks, and end of study treatment. Thyroid function tests (free tri-iodothyronine [T3], free thyroxine [T4], and thyroid-stimulating hormone [TSH]) were done at baseline. TSH was then assessed at weeks 2, 4, 8, and 12, and every 8 weeks thereafter. Free T3/T4 was assessed as clinically indicated. Safety was assessed throughout the study, with adverse events graded according to CTCAE. The primary endpoint was progression-free survival— time from randomisation to objective tumour progression or death—assessed by independent review committee. Secondary endpoints were assessing the proportion of patients who achieved an objective response, response duration, overall survival, safety, and patient-reported outcomes. Objective response rate (assessed by independent review committee) was defined as the proportion of patients with confirmed (persistence on repeat imaging after at least 4 weeks) complete or partial response as per RECIST criteria. 15 Patient-reported outcomes were assessed on day 1 of treatment, every 4 weeks during treatment, at the end of study treatment, and 28 days after last dose of study treatment. Assessments used the Functional Assessment of Cancer Therapy–Kidney Symptom Index (FKSI)-15,¹⁷ FKSI-Disease Related Symptoms (FKSI-DRS) subscale,¹⁸ and European Quality of Life-5 Dimensions (EQ-5D) questionnaires.¹⁹ FKSI-15 consists of 15 items; FKSI-DRS 1288 www.thelancet.com/oncology Vol 14 December 2013 Articles consists of nine of the 15 items that focus on kidney cancer-specific symptoms. Higher scores reflected a better health state. The EQ-5D is a brief, general health- status questionnaire consisting of five questions on current health state and one visual analogue scale summary item on overall health status. Statistical analysis The trial tested the null hypothesis that median progression-free survival with axitinib was 5.5 months versus the alternative hypothesis that it was at least 9.8 months (78% improvement), corresponding to a hazard ratio (HR) of 0.56 (overall one-sided a=0.025). 148 progression-free survival events were needed for a log-rank test with an overall one-sided significance level of 0.025 to have 90% power. An estimated total of 247 patients were to be enrolled. Efficacy and patient-reported outcomes were analysed in the intention-to-treat (ITT) population (all treatment- naive patients randomly assigned to groups); safety was analysed in patients who received at least one dose of study drug. A third-party independent data monitoring committee monitored safety on a periodic basis as well as interim efficacy. At the interim futility analysis, planned when 74 patients had investigator-assessed disease progression or death (half of those required for final analysis), the data monitoring committee could recommend early trial stopping, or increase the number of patients required to be observed with disease progression or death for final analysis by up to 48, based on conditional power (≥73% and <90%). The Kaplan-Meier method was used to estimate median progression-free survival; two-sided 95% CIs were calculated. Progression-free survival was compared with a stratified, one-sided, log-rank test. Cox proportional hazard model explored the potential influence of ECOG performance status and patient characteristics (ie, age, sex, ethnic origin, and Memorial Sloan-Kettering Cancer Center [MSKCC] risk group) on progression-free survival. Estimated HR and two-sided 95% CI were provided. A test of the proportional hazards assumption concluded that there was no violation of that assumption. Additionally, median progression-free survival, HRs, and two-sided 95% CIs were provided for each level of ECOG performance status and baseline characteristics. The Cochran-Mantel-Haenszel test was used for stratified analyses comparing the proportion of patients achieving an objective response between treatments. The Kaplan-Meier method was used to estimate median duration of response; two-sided 95% CIs were calculated. Final analysis of overall survival requires 178 deaths; these data are currently immature. Patients are being followed up for survival every 3 months after study treatment discontinuation. Adverse events and laboratory abnormalities were reported. Mean (SD) and median FKSI-15, FKSI-DRS, EQ-5D scores and changes from baseline were reported. A repeated-measures mixed-effects model compared patient-reported outcomes between treatments; variables included treatment, time, and treatment-by-time, with baseline as covariate. Minimally important differences were prespecified as five or more points for FKSI-15 and three or more points for FKSI-DRS, based on published reports. 17,18 Survival analysis methods evaluated time-to- deterioration composite endpoints, where time-to- deterioration was a composite definition consisting of time to death, tumour progression, or meaningful worsening in quality of life (defined by minimally important difference), whichever occurred first. SAS version 9.2 was used for all statistical analyses. The study is registered at ClinicalTrials.gov, NCT00920816. **Role of the funding source** The study sponsor was involved in study design, data collection, and analysis. All authors reviewed and approved the final content of this report. All authors had access to the data in this report. The corresponding author had full access to all data and final responsibility to submit for publication. ## Results Between June 14, 2010, and April 21, 2011, 288 patients with treatment-naive metastatic renal-cell carcinoma were enrolled. The highest enrolment occurred in Ukraine (n=61), Russia (n=58), and India (n=34); with enrolment occurring in ten other countries (appendix). Patient demographic and baseline characteristics were balanced between treatment groups (table 1). 192 patients were randomly assigned to axitinib and 96 to sorafenib (figure 1). Three patients assigned to axitinib did not receive treatment and were not included in the safety population. The interim analysis occurred after 80 (54%) of the required 148 patients died or had disease progression as per investigator assessment, at which time the data monitoring committee recommended increasing the required number of patients with disease progression, per independent review committee assessment, or death to 169. The cutoff date for the analyses reported here was July 27, 2012, when 171 (59%) of 288 patients died or had disease progression, as assessed by the independent review committee. As of that date, 125 (65%) of 192 and 69 (72%) of 96 patients had discontinued axitinib and sorafenib, respectively (figure 1). The primary endpoint of median progression-free survival, as assessed by independent review committee, was 10.1 months (95% CI 7.2–12.1) with axitinib and 6.5 months (4.7–8.3) with sorafenib (figure 2A). This difference was not significant (stratified HR 0.77, 95% CI 0.56–1.05; one-sided p=0.038). In a prespecified analysis, median progression-free survival was longer with axitinib versus sorafenib in patients with ECOG performance status 0 (figure 2B), but not in patients with ECOG performance status 1 (figure 2C). There were no significant differences in other prespecified analyses (figure 3). In a post-hoc analysis of 250 patients with previous nephrectomy, median progression-free survival as assessed by independent review committee was also improved with axitinib versus sorafenib (figure 2D). More patients had an objective response, as assessed by independent review committee, with axitinib than with sorafenib (62 [32%] of 192 vs 14 [15%] of 96; risk ratio 2.21, 95% CI 1.31-3.75, stratified one-sided p=0.0006; table 2). No patient had a complete response with either treatment. Median duration of response was 14.7 months (95% CI 11.0-not estimable [NE]) with axitinib and 14.3 months (11.3-NE) with sorafenib. Median treatment duration was longer (373 days [range 4-743] vs 303.5 days [range 6-646]) and median relative dose intensity (total dose administered / total dose assigned×100) was greater (125% vs 98%) with axitinib than with sorafenib. The axitinib dose was increased above 10 mg daily in 134 (71%) of 189 patients; 29 (15%) patients received 12-14 mg daily (median 28 days [range 2-498]), and 105 (56%) patients received 20 mg daily (median 168 days [range 5-640]). A lower proportion of patients treated with axitinib had dose reductions (47 [25%] of 189 vs 41 [43%] of 96), or interruptions (136 [72%] of 189 vs 75 [78%] of 96) than those treated with sorafenib. Adverse events led to dose interruptions in 94 (50%) of 189 patients receiving axitinib and 44 (46%) of 96 receiving sorafenib. Five (3%) of 189 patients discontinued axitinib and two (2%) of 96 discontinued sorafenib because of a treatment-related adverse event. The most frequent all-grade, all-causality adverse events were diarrhoea, hypertension, weight decrease, and fatigue with axitinib versus diarrhoea, palmar- plantar erythrodysaesthesia (PPE), and hypertension with sorafenib (table 3). Adverse events more common (10% or greater difference) with axitinib versus sorafenib were diarrhoea, hypertension, weight decrease, decreased appetite, dysphonia, and hypothyroidism (table 3), and upper abdominal pain (31 [16%] of 189 vs six [6%] of 96 patients); those more common with sorafenib versus axitinib included PPE and rash (table 3), alopecia (18 [19%] of 96 vs eight [4%] of 189), and erythema (18 [19%] of 96 vs five [3%] of 189). The most frequently reported grade 3 or 4 adverse events were hypertension, 1290 www.thelancet.com/oncology Vol 14 December 2013 Articles diarrhoea, weight decrease, and asthenia in axitinib-treated patients and PPE, diarrhoea, and asthenia in sorafenib-treated patients (table 3). Grade 3 or worse all-causality adverse events more common (5% or greater difference) in patients treated with axitinib were hypertension and weight decrease; PPE was more common in those treated with sorafenib. All-causality serious adverse events were reported in 64 (34%) of 189 patients receiving axitinib and 24 (25%) of 96 patients receiving sorafenib, including ten (5%) and seven (7%) patients, respectively, with treatment-related serious adverse events. The most common all-causality serious adverse events excluding disease progression were diarrhoea (five [3%]), pleural effusion (three [2%]), and cardiac arrest (three [2%]) in the 189 patients in the axitinib group and abdominal pain (three [3%]), and pleural effusion (three [3%]) in the 96 patients in the sorafenib group. Serious treatment-related adverse events in the 189 axitinib-treated patients included diarrhoea (four [2%] patients) and atrial flutter, cardiac arrest, myocardial infarction, gastric ulcer, gastrointestinal haemorrhage, melaena, nausea, rectal haemorrhage, vomiting, retroperitoneal haematoma, asthenia, mucosal inflammation, dehydration, decreased appetite, aortoduodenal fistula, and hypertensive crisis (one [<1%] patient each). Serious treatment-related adverse events in the 96 sorafenib-treated patients were acute myocardial infarction, abdominal pain, diarrhoea, nausea, impaired healing, bacterial skin infection, pleural effusion, erythema multiforme, and PPE (one [1%] patient each). erythema multiforme, and PPE (one [1%] patient each). At data cutoff, 78 (41%) of 192 patients in the axitinib group and 36 (38%) of 96 in the sorafenib group had died. The most common cause of death was disease progression (67 [86%] patients in the axitinib group and 35 [97%] in the sorafenib group). One patient in the axitinib group died of treatment-related cardiac arrest; no patients in the sorafenib group had a treatment-related death. Additional causes of death were either unknown (six [8%] in the axitinib group) or other (four [5%] in the axitinib group and one [3%] in the sorafenib group). However, at the end of treatment when most patients discontinued because of progression, mean FKSI-15 (39.1 [SD 9.1] for axitinib and 39.5 [SD 8.9] for sorafenib) and FKSI-DRS (26.6 [SD 5.5] for axitinib and 26.8 [SD 6.0] for sorafenib) scores worsened in both groups. No significant differences were noted between axitinib and sorafenib in median time-to-deterioration composite endpoints, using FKSI-15 (5·3 months [95% CI 4·5-6·5] _vs_ 4·7 months [4·4-6·5]; unstratified HR 1·00, 95% CI 0·76-1·32; one-sided p=0·51) or FKSI-DRS (6·3 months [95% CI 4·5-6·5] _vs_ 4·7 months [4·4-6·5]; unstratified HR 0·98; 95% CI 0·74-1·29; one-sided p=0·43). For EQ-5D, mean baseline (0·71 [SD 0·25] _vs_ 0·71 [0·27]) and end-of-treatment (0·64 [SD 0·27] _vs_ 0·59 [0·29]) scores were similar with axitinib versus sorafenib. Repeated-measures mixed-effects analyses revealed no significant difference between the two treatments in overall scores with respect to FKSI-15, FKSI-DRS, or EQ-5D (data not shown). ## Discussion In this randomised phase 3 trial comparing axitinib versus sorafenib as first-line therapy, although median progression-free survival was numerically longer with axitinib than with sorafenib, the difference was not significant. The trial was designed with the prediction of an ambitious 4·3-month (78%) improvement in median progression-free survival over the active comparator sorafenib. Sorafenib showed longer median progression- free survival than expected, ⁶,¹³ and axitinib showed shorter median progression-free survival than previously observed in patients with cytokine-refractory metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. ¹¹,¹² In a prespecified analysis, we observed a 7-1-month difference in median progression-free survival with axitinib versus sorafenib in patients with ECOG perfor- mance status 0, but no difference in patients with per- formance status 1. To verify that patients with ECOG performance status 1 were accurately classified, and met entry criteria, baseline ECOG performance status was audited at five of the highest enrolling sites; no obvious evidence of ineligibility based on this measure was noted from patients' medical records. A similar trend in ECOG performance status subgroup analysis was observed in the TIVO-1 study. 21 Patients with ECOG performance status 0 treated with tivozanib had significantly longer median progression-free survival compared to those treated with sorafenib (14.8 months vs 9.1 months, respectively; two-sided p=0.004); however, no difference was observed between the two treatment groups in patients with ECOG performance status 1 (9.1 months vs 9.0 months, respectively; two-sided p=0.59). Median progression-free survival with sorafenib was similar in patients with ECOG performance status 0 and 1 in both TIVO-121 (9.1 months for patients with performance status 0 and 9.0 months for those with performance status 1) and the present trial (6.6 months 1292 www.thelancet.com/oncology Vol 14 December 2013 Articles for patients with performance status 0 and 6·4 months for those with performance status 1). It is intriguing that ECOG performance status seemed to affect progression- free survival in patients receiving axitinib or tivozanib, but not in those receiving sorafenib. This pattern was not noted in the global phase 3 AXIS trial⁸ that compared second-line axitinib versus sorafenib in patients with metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. A large proportion of patients in AXIS were recruited from North America and western Europe. By contrast, few patients in the current trial were recruited from those areas. Instead, more than half of the patients in this trial were enrolled from eastern Europe, where resource limitations and local practice standards might have affected the type of patient enrolled, or patient management. Patients from lower socioeconomic regions might have had increased disease burdens due to delays in both diagnosis and treatment, thus possibly worsening progression-free survival outcomes, especially in patients with ECOG performance status 1. Another hypothesis might be that eastern European patients who have access to clinical trials might have a higher socioeconomic status, thus leading to increased compliance and better than expected outcomes in patients with ECOG performance status 0. However it is worth noting that ECOG performance status 0 and 1 are broad categories that allow much clinical latitude and remain somewhat subjective. As improved clinical²² and molecular²³ prognostic categories are developed, older classifications (eg, ECOG performance status) are likely to be replaced with more objective ones in future clinical trials. Although caution should be exercised when comparing across trials, the AXIS⁸ trial appeared to include more patients with MSKCC poor risk than our trial; however, this difference might be explained by use of two different MSKCC risk criteria, based on line of therapy, in the present trial²⁰ versus the AXIS trial.²⁴ More patients in the present trial had axitinib dose increases, and fewer had axitinib dose reductions, compared with patients in the AXIS trial.⁸ This difference might be due to patients receiving first-line versus second-line therapy being able to better tolerate dose increases, and to differences between one of the axitinib dose-escalation criteria (in the AXIS trial, dose increases were not allowed in patients receiving antihypertensive therapy, whereas they were allowed here). This trial has shown that axitinib has clinical activity in the first-line treatment of metastatic renal-cell carcinoma (panel). At the cutoff date for our analysis, only 114 (40%) of 288 patients had died, and median overall survival had not been reached. We continue to follow patients for overall survival in the study, and plan to report the definitive results when they are mature and more interpretable. The safety profiles for axitinib and sorafenib in the present study were in line with adverse events reported in previously treated patients in AXIS, 8 and those reported with other VEGFR inhibitors in "**Panel: Research in context**\n\n**Systematic review**\nA systematic literature review was not done as part of the planning for this trial. However, clinical trial data had shown median progression-free survival of 13·7 months with axitinib in cytokine-refractory metastatic renal-cell carcinoma\u003Csup\u003E11,12\u003C/sup\u003E and median progression-free survival of 5·5 months\u003Csup\u003E6\u003C/sup\u003E and 5·7 months\u003Csup\u003E13\u003C/sup\u003E with sorafenib in cytokine-refractory and treatment-naive disease, respectively. On the basis of these results, we amended a randomised trial of second-line axitinib versus sorafenib in Asian patients with advanced renal-cell carcinoma to include a separate global treatment-naive population." **Interpretation** To our knowledge, this is the first phase 3 trial evaluating axitinib, a VEGF-receptor inhibitor, in patients with treatment-naive metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. Axitinib was found to have clinical activity, and a manageable safety profile in these patients. Median progression-free survival with axitinib in the overall population is similar to that reported in other first-line studies evaluating sunitinib⁵ or pazopanib⁷ for the treatment of metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. Given its clinical activity and differentiated toxicity profile, axitinib is a welcome addition to the treatment armamentarium for patients with metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. treatment-naive patients.^(5,7,13,21,25) Adverse event profiles differed between agents. Baseline mean FKSI-15 and FKSI-DRS scores in the present trial were similar to those observed in a general US population,26 and so improvement in quality of life was not expected. Maintenance of quality of life was regarded as a good outcome. A consistent finding in observed mean FKSI-15 and FKSI-DRS scores was shown here as in the AXIS trial,27 in which quality of life worsened at the end of treatment with both axitinib and sorafenib, presumably because disease progression worsened quality of life. In the longitudinal analysis, postbaseline scores for patient-reported outcomes were similar between axitinib and sorafenib over time, consistent with results from AXIS.27 Unlike the AXIS trial,8 although the time-to-deterioration composite endpoints of time to death, tumour progression, or meaningful worsening in quality of life appeared longer for axitinib versus sorafenib, the differences were not significant; thus for both patient groups clinical efficacy (progression-free survival) was achieved without sacrificing quality of life. This study had several limitations. The sample size was relatively small, designed to detect a large magnitude of difference. Sorafenib, the comparator in this trial, was chosen because of its activity and broad indication in advanced renal-cell carcinoma,¹⁶ as well as its availability in the regions where the trial was undertaken and experience with its use in the AXIS trial.⁸ In retrospect, this choice might have been a limitation of the present study in that axitinib was not compared with what are regarded as standard first-line drugs, sunitinib or pazopanib.²⁵ Additionally, patients and investigators were unmasked to treatment, which might have led to bias in safety and assessment of patient-reported outcomes; efficacy endpoints, however, were assessed by masked www.thelancet.com/oncology Vol 14 December 2013 1293 Articles independent review committee. Selection bias due to use of permuted blocks for generation of randomisation lists is another potential limitation; however, the effect is thought to be minimal since the investigators did not have access to the central randomisation list (used for all centres) and, hence, would not have been able to predict treatment assignment. To our knowledge, this is the first randomised phase 3 trial comparing axitinib with an active agent in the first-line setting. Although there was no significant difference between patient groups' progression-free survival, axitinib showed clinical activity and an acceptable safety profile as first-line therapy for patients with treatment-naive metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. Given that no therapy is curative for patients with this disease, continuing research is needed to identify new active drugs. Articles Axitinib versus sorafenib as first-line therapy in patients with metastatic renal-cell carcinoma: a randomised open-label phase 3 trial CrossMark Thomas E Hutson, Vladimir Lesovoy, Salman Al-Shukri, Viktor P Stus, Oleg N Lipatov, Angel H Bair, Brad Rosbrook, Connie Chen, Sinil Kim, Nicholas J Vogelzang Summary Background In previous clinical trials of patients with metastatic renal-cell carcinoma, patients treated with axitinib as second-line therapy had longer median progression-free survival than those treated with sorafenib. We therefore undertook a phase 3 trial comparing axitinib with sorafenib in patients with treatment-naive metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. **Methods** In this randomised, open-label, phase 3 trial, patients with treatment-naive, measurable, clear-cell metastatic renal-cell carcinoma from 13 countries were stratified by Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status, and then randomly assigned (2:1) by a centralised registration system to receive axitinib 5 mg twice daily, or sorafenib 400 mg twice daily. The primary endpoint was progression-free survival, assessed by masked independent review committee in the intention-to-treat population. This ongoing trial is registered at ClinicalTrials.gov, NCT00920816. Findings Between June 14, 2010, and April 21, 2011, we randomly assigned 192 patients to receive axitinib, and 96 patients to receive sorafenib. The cutoff date for this analysis was July 27, 2012, when 171 (59%) of 288 patients died or had disease progression, as assessed by the independent review committee. There was no significant difference in median progression-free survival between patients treated with axitinib or sorafenib (10.1 months [95% CI 7.2–12.1] vs 6.5 months [4.7–8.3], respectively; stratified hazard ratio 0.77, 95% CI 0.56–1.05). Any-grade adverse events that were more common (≥10% difference) with axitinib than with sorafenib were diarrhoea (94 [50%] of 189 patients vs 38 [40%] of 96 patients), hypertension (92 [49%] vs 28 [29%]), weight decrease (69 [37%] vs 23 [24%]), decreased appetite (54 [29%] vs 18 [19%]), dysphonia (44 [23%] vs ten [10%]), hypothyroidism (39 [21%] vs seven [7%]), and upper abdominal pain (31 [16%] vs six [6%]); those more common with sorafenib than with axitinib included palmar-plantar erythrodysaesthesia (PPE; 37 [39%] of 96 patients vs 50 [26%] of 189), rash (19 [20%] vs 18 [10%]), alopecia (18 [19%] vs eight [4%]), and erythema (18 [19%] vs five [3%]). The most common grade 3 or 4 adverse events in patients treated with axitinib included hypertension (26 [14%] of 189 patients), diarrhoea (17 [9%]), asthenia (16 [8%]), weight decrease (16 [8%]), and PPE (14 [7%]); common grade 3 or 4 adverse events in patients treated with sorafenib included PPE (15 [16%] of 96 patients), diarrhoea (five [5%]), and asthenia (five [5%]). Serious adverse events were reported in 64 (34%) of 189 patients receiving axitinib, and 24 (25%) of 96 patients receiving sorafenib. Interpretation Axitinib did not significantly increase progression-free survival in patients with treatment-naive metastatic renal-cell carcinoma compared with those treated with sorafenib, but did demonstrate clinical activity and an acceptable safety profile. Funding Pfizer Inc. # Introduction The survival of patients with metastatic clear-cell renal-cell carcinoma has improved with the availability of drugs targeting VEGF, or its receptors (VEGFRs), and mTOR. Currently approved and available mTOR inhibitors include temsirolimus¹ and everolimus;² VEGF/VEGFR-targeted drugs are bevacizumab,³,⁴ sunitinib,⁵ sorafenib,⁶ pazopanib,⁷ and axitinib.⁸ Axitinib (Pfizer Inc, manufactured in Freiburg, Germany), a potent and selective second-generation VEGFR inhibitor,⁹ is approved in the USA,¹⁰ European Union, and elsewhere for treatment of advanced renal-cell carcinoma after failure of one previous systemic therapy. Previously, trials showed median progression-free survival of 13.7 months with axitinib in cytokine-refractory metastatic renal-cell carcin- oma, 11,12 and median progression-free survival of 5.5 months and 5.7 months with sorafenib in cytokine- refractory and treatment-naive disease, 13 respectively. On the basis of these data, an ongoing randomised registrational trial (NCT00920816) of second-line axitinib versus sorafenib in Asian patients 14 was amended to add a separate global treatment-naive population that would be analysed independently from the second-line population. Sorafenib was chosen as the comparator because it was available in the regions where the trial was being done. Here, we report results from the treatment-naive population. I.I. Mechnikov", Dnipropetrovs'k, Ukraine (Prof V P Stus MD); Republican Clinical Oncology Dispensary, Ufa, Russia (Prof O N Lipatov MD); Pfizer Oncology, San Diego, CA, USA (A H Bair PhD, B Rosbrook MS, S Kim MD); Pfizer Oncology, New York, NY, USA (C Chen PharmD); and Comprehensive Cancer Centers of Nevada, Las Vegas, NV, USA, and US Oncology Research, Houston, TX, USA (Prof N J Vogelzang MD) Correspondence to: Prof Thomas E Hutson, Baylor Sammons Cancer Center, Dallas, TX 75246, USA thomas.hutson@usoncology. com www.thelancet.com/oncology Vol 14 December 2013 1287 Articles See Online for appendix # Methods ## Study design and patients This phase 3 randomised, open-label trial evaluated efficacy, safety, and patient-reported outcomes for axitinib versus sorafenib in patients with treatment-naive metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. Patients for this trial were recruited from 13 countries (appendix). Key eligibility criteria included: aged 18 years or older; histologically or cytologically confirmed metastatic renal-cell carcinoma with a clear-cell component; unidimensionally measurable disease by Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumors (RECIST, version 1.0); 15 Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status 0 or 1; at least 2 weeks since previous radiotherapy or surgery; adequate renal, hepatic, and haematological organ function; and life expectancy of at least 12 weeks. Antihypertensive therapy was allowed. Previous nephrectomy was not required. Previous systemic therapy for metastatic renal-cell carcinoma was not allowed; adjuvant interferon or interleukin, or both, were allowed if recurrence occurred more than 6 months after completion. Other exclusion factors included having uncontrolled hypertension (blood pressure higher than 140/90 mm Hg); brain metastases or CNS involvement; a history of other malignancy; or a significant medical history such as myocardial infarction, cerebrovascular accident, or transient ischaemic attack within the past 12 months. The trial was undertaken in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki, the International Conference on Harmonisation guidelines on Good Clinical Practice, and applicable local regulatory requirements and laws. All patients provided written informed consent. The protocol, amendments, and informed consent forms were approved by an institutional review board or independent ethics committee at each study centre. **Randomisation and masking** Patients were stratified according to ECOG performance status (0 or 1), and were randomly assigned in a 2:1 ratio by a centralised registration system to receive either axitinib 5 mg twice daily, or sorafenib 400 mg twice daily. Randomisation sequences, used by all centres, were generated by the central randomisation administrator using permuted blocks with a block size of six (four for axitinib to two for sorafenib) within each performance status stratum. Treatment group assignment for an individual patient was obtained at the time of enrolment by the investigating site via web interface in a manner that concealed all past and future assignments. Patients and investigators were unmasked to treatment. Radiologists participating in independent review committee assessment of the primary endpoint were masked to treatment. ## Procedures Axitinib was given orally at a starting dose of 5 mg twice daily with food, and sorafenib was given orally at a starting dose of 400 mg twice daily without food, both in 4-week cycles. Axitinib doses could be increased first to 7 mg twice daily, and subsequently to 10 mg twice daily, for patients who had not had any grade two or worse treatment-related adverse events (as defined by Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events, or CTCAE, version 3.0) for at least 2 weeks and had blood pressure 150/90 mm Hg or lower. On the basis of adverse events, or laboratory abnormalities, axitinib dose could be reduced to 3 mg twice daily, and then 2 mg twice daily, and sorafenib dose could be reduced to 400 mg once daily, and then 400 mg every other day. Both drugs were given according to their approved labelling, such that dose increases were allowed with axitinib,¹º but not with sorafenib.¹⁶ Patients who had disease progression but who had clinical benefit from axitinib or sorafenib were eligible for continued treatment. Crossover between the two drugs was not allowed during the study. Baseline tumour assessments included CT or MRI scans of brain, chest, abdomen, and pelvis, and a bone scan. CT or MRI (not including brain) and bone scans (only if baseline lesions were present) were repeated at week 6, week 12, every 8 weeks thereafter, and end of study treatment. All CT, MRI, and bone scans were centrally reviewed by an independent review committee. Physical examinations were done at baseline, day 1 (if day 1 was more than 7 days since baseline), week 2, week 4, every 4 weeks thereafter, and end of study treatment. Blood pressure was monitored at each clinic visit, and twice a day at home by patients. Assessment of ECOG performance status and laboratory tests for haematology, blood chemistry, and urinalysis occurred at baseline, day 1, every 4 weeks, and end of study treatment. Thyroid function tests (free tri-iodothyronine [T3], free thyroxine [T4], and thyroid-stimulating hormone [TSH]) were done at baseline. TSH was then assessed at weeks 2, 4, 8, and 12, and every 8 weeks thereafter. Free T3/T4 was assessed as clinically indicated. Safety was assessed throughout the study, with adverse events graded according to CTCAE. The primary endpoint was progression-free survival— time from randomisation to objective tumour progression or death—assessed by independent review committee. Secondary endpoints were assessing the proportion of patients who achieved an objective response, response duration, overall survival, safety, and patient-reported outcomes. Objective response rate (assessed by independent review committee) was defined as the proportion of patients with confirmed (persistence on repeat imaging after at least 4 weeks) complete or partial response as per RECIST criteria. 15 Patient-reported outcomes were assessed on day 1 of treatment, every 4 weeks during treatment, at the end of study treatment, and 28 days after last dose of study treatment. Assessments used the Functional Assessment of Cancer Therapy–Kidney Symptom Index (FKSI)-15,¹⁷ FKSI-Disease Related Symptoms (FKSI-DRS) subscale,¹⁸ and European Quality of Life-5 Dimensions (EQ-5D) questionnaires.¹⁹ FKSI-15 consists of 15 items; FKSI-DRS 1288 www.thelancet.com/oncology Vol 14 December 2013 Articles consists of nine of the 15 items that focus on kidney cancer-specific symptoms. Higher scores reflected a better health state. The EQ-5D is a brief, general health- status questionnaire consisting of five questions on current health state and one visual analogue scale summary item on overall health status. Statistical analysis The trial tested the null hypothesis that median progression-free survival with axitinib was 5.5 months versus the alternative hypothesis that it was at least 9.8 months (78% improvement), corresponding to a hazard ratio (HR) of 0.56 (overall one-sided a=0.025). 148 progression-free survival events were needed for a log-rank test with an overall one-sided significance level of 0.025 to have 90% power. An estimated total of 247 patients were to be enrolled. Efficacy and patient-reported outcomes were analysed in the intention-to-treat (ITT) population (all treatment- naive patients randomly assigned to groups); safety was analysed in patients who received at least one dose of study drug. A third-party independent data monitoring committee monitored safety on a periodic basis as well as interim efficacy. At the interim futility analysis, planned when 74 patients had investigator-assessed disease progression or death (half of those required for final analysis), the data monitoring committee could recommend early trial stopping, or increase the number of patients required to be observed with disease progression or death for final analysis by up to 48, based on conditional power (≥73% and <90%). The Kaplan-Meier method was used to estimate median progression-free survival; two-sided 95% CIs were calculated. Progression-free survival was compared with a stratified, one-sided, log-rank test. Cox proportional hazard model explored the potential influence of ECOG performance status and patient characteristics (ie, age, sex, ethnic origin, and Memorial Sloan-Kettering Cancer Center [MSKCC] risk group) on progression-free survival. Estimated HR and two-sided 95% CI were provided. A test of the proportional hazards assumption concluded that there was no violation of that assumption. Additionally, median progression-free survival, HRs, and two-sided 95% CIs were provided for each level of ECOG performance status and baseline characteristics. The Cochran-Mantel-Haenszel test was used for stratified analyses comparing the proportion of patients achieving an objective response between treatments. The Kaplan-Meier method was used to estimate median duration of response; two-sided 95% CIs were calculated. Final analysis of overall survival requires 178 deaths; these data are currently immature. Patients are being followed up for survival every 3 months after study treatment discontinuation. Adverse events and laboratory abnormalities were reported. Mean (SD) and median FKSI-15, FKSI-DRS, EQ-5D scores and changes from baseline were reported. A repeated-measures mixed-effects model compared patient-reported outcomes between treatments; variables included treatment, time, and treatment-by-time, with baseline as covariate. Minimally important differences were prespecified as five or more points for FKSI-15 and three or more points for FKSI-DRS, based on published reports. 17,18 Survival analysis methods evaluated time-to- deterioration composite endpoints, where time-to- deterioration was a composite definition consisting of time to death, tumour progression, or meaningful worsening in quality of life (defined by minimally important difference), whichever occurred first. SAS version 9.2 was used for all statistical analyses. The study is registered at ClinicalTrials.gov, NCT00920816. **Role of the funding source** The study sponsor was involved in study design, data collection, and analysis. The corresponding author had full access to all data and final responsibility to submit for publication. ## Results Between June 14, 2010, and April 21, 2011, 288 patients with treatment-naive metastatic renal-cell carcinoma were enrolled. The highest enrolment occurred in Ukraine (n=61), Russia (n=58), and India (n=34); with enrolment occurring in ten other countries (appendix). Patient demographic and baseline characteristics were balanced between treatment groups (table 1). 192 patients were randomly assigned to axitinib and 96 to sorafenib (figure 1). Three patients assigned to axitinib did not receive treatment and were not included in the safety population. The interim analysis occurred after 80 (54%) of the required 148 patients died or had disease progression as per investigator assessment, at which time the data monitoring committee recommended increasing the required number of patients with disease progression, per independent review committee assessment, or death to 169. The cutoff date for the analyses reported here was July 27, 2012, when 171 (59%) of 288 patients died or had disease progression, as assessed by the independent review committee. As of that date, 125 (65%) of 192 and 69 (72%) of 96 patients had discontinued axitinib and sorafenib, respectively (figure 1).
