Data are not available for median follow-up. The primary endpoint of median progression-free survival, as assessed by independent review committee, was 10.1 months (95% CI 7.2–12.1) with axitinib and 6.5 months (4.7–8.3) with sorafenib (figure 2A). This difference was not significant (stratified HR 0.77, 95% CI 0.56–1.05; one-sided p=0.038). In a prespecified analysis, median progression-free survival was longer with axitinib versus sorafenib in patients with ECOG performance status 0 (figure 2B), but not in patients with ECOG performance status 1 (figure 2C). There were no significant differences in other prespecified analyses (figure 3). In a post-hoc analysis of 250 patients with previous nephrectomy, median progression-free survival as assessed by independent review committee was also improved with axitinib versus sorafenib (figure 2D). More patients had an objective response, as assessed by independent review committee, with axitinib than with sorafenib (62 [32%] of 192 vs 14 [15%] of 96; risk ratio 2.21, 95% CI 1.31-3.75, stratified one-sided p=0.0006; table 2). No patient had a complete response with either treatment. Median duration of response was 14.7 months (95% CI 11.0-not estimable [NE]) with axitinib and 14.3 months (11.3-NE) with sorafenib. Median treatment duration was longer (373 days [range 4-743] vs 303.5 days [range 6-646]) and median relative dose intensity (total dose administered / total dose assigned×100) was greater (125% vs 98%) with axitinib than with sorafenib. The axitinib dose was increased above 10 mg daily in 134 (71%) of 189 patients; 29 (15%) patients received 12-14 mg daily (median 28 days [range 2-498]), and 105 (56%) patients received 20 mg daily (median 168 days [range 5-640]). A lower proportion of patients treated with axitinib had dose reductions (47 [25%] of 189 vs 41 [43%] of 96), or interruptions (136 [72%] of 189 vs 75 [78%] of 96) than those treated with sorafenib. Adverse events led to dose interruptions in 94 (50%) of 189 patients receiving axitinib and 44 (46%) of 96 receiving sorafenib. Five (3%) of 189 patients discontinued axitinib and two (2%) of 96 discontinued sorafenib because of a treatment-related adverse event. The most frequent all-grade, all-causality adverse events were diarrhoea, hypertension, weight decrease, and fatigue with axitinib versus diarrhoea, palmar- plantar erythrodysaesthesia (PPE), and hypertension with sorafenib (table 3). Adverse events more common (10% or greater difference) with axitinib versus sorafenib were diarrhoea, hypertension, weight decrease, decreased appetite, dysphonia, and hypothyroidism (table 3), and upper abdominal pain (31 [16%] of 189 vs six [6%] of 96 patients); those more common with sorafenib versus axitinib included PPE and rash (table 3), alopecia (18 [19%] of 96 vs eight [4%] of 189), and erythema (18 [19%] of 96 vs five [3%] of 189). The most frequently reported grade 3 or 4 adverse events were hypertension, 1290 www.thelancet.com/oncology Vol 14 December 2013 Articles diarrhoea, weight decrease, and asthenia in axitinib-treated patients and PPE, diarrhoea, and asthenia in sorafenib-treated patients (table 3). Grade 3 or worse all-causality adverse events more common (5% or greater difference) in patients treated with axitinib were hypertension and weight decrease; PPE was more common in those treated with sorafenib. All-causality serious adverse events were reported in 64 (34%) of 189 patients receiving axitinib and 24 (25%) of 96 patients receiving sorafenib, including ten (5%) and seven (7%) patients, respectively, with treatment-related serious adverse events. The most common all-causality serious adverse events excluding disease progression were diarrhoea (five [3%]), pleural effusion (three [2%]), and cardiac arrest (three [2%]) in the 189 patients in the axitinib group and abdominal pain (three [3%]), and pleural effusion (three [3%]) in the 96 patients in the sorafenib group. However, at the end of treatment when most patients discontinued because of progression, mean FKSI-15 (39.1 [SD 9.1] for axitinib and 39.5 [SD 8.9] for sorafenib) and FKSI-DRS (26.6 [SD 5.5] for axitinib and 26.8 [SD 6.0] for sorafenib) scores worsened in both groups. No significant differences were noted between axitinib and sorafenib in median time-to-deterioration composite endpoints, using FKSI-15 (5·3 months [95% CI 4·5-6·5] _vs_ 4·7 months [4·4-6·5]; unstratified HR 1·00, 95% CI 0·76-1·32; one-sided p=0·51) or FKSI-DRS (6·3 months [95% CI 4·5-6·5] _vs_ 4·7 months [4·4-6·5]; unstratified HR 0·98; 95% CI 0·74-1·29; one-sided p=0·43). For EQ-5D, mean baseline (0·71 [SD 0·25] _vs_ 0·71 [0·27]) and end-of-treatment (0·64 [SD 0·27] _vs_ 0·59 [0·29]) scores were similar with axitinib versus sorafenib. Repeated-measures mixed-effects analyses revealed no significant difference between the two treatments in overall scores with respect to FKSI-15, FKSI-DRS, or EQ-5D (data not shown). ## Discussion In this randomised phase 3 trial comparing axitinib versus sorafenib as first-line therapy, although median progression-free survival was numerically longer with axitinib than with sorafenib, the difference was not significant. The trial was designed with the prediction of an ambitious 4·3-month (78%) improvement in median progression-free survival over the active comparator sorafenib. Sorafenib showed longer median progression- free survival than expected, ⁶,¹³ and axitinib showed shorter median progression-free survival than previously observed in patients with cytokine-refractory metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. ¹¹,¹² In a prespecified analysis, we observed a 7-1-month difference in median progression-free survival with axitinib versus sorafenib in patients with ECOG perfor- mance status 0, but no difference in patients with per- formance status 1. To verify that patients with ECOG performance status 1 were accurately classified, and met entry criteria, baseline ECOG performance status was audited at five of the highest enrolling sites; no obvious evidence of ineligibility based on this measure was noted from patients' medical records. A similar trend in ECOG performance status subgroup analysis was observed in the TIVO-1 study. 21 Patients with ECOG performance status 0 treated with tivozanib had significantly longer median progression-free survival compared to those treated with sorafenib (14.8 months vs 9.1 months, respectively; two-sided p=0.004); however, no difference was observed between the two treatment groups in patients with ECOG performance status 1 (9.1 months vs 9.0 months, respectively; two-sided p=0.59). Median progression-free survival with sorafenib was similar in patients with ECOG performance status 0 and 1 in both TIVO-121 (9.1 months for patients with performance status 0 and 9.0 months for those with performance status 1) and the present trial (6.6 months 1292 www.thelancet.com/oncology Vol 14 December 2013 Articles for patients with performance status 0 and 6·4 months for those with performance status 1). It is intriguing that ECOG performance status seemed to affect progression- free survival in patients receiving axitinib or tivozanib, but not in those receiving sorafenib. This pattern was not noted in the global phase 3 AXIS trial⁸ that compared second-line axitinib versus sorafenib in patients with metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. A large proportion of patients in AXIS were recruited from North America and western Europe. By contrast, few patients in the current trial were recruited from those areas. Instead, more than half of the patients in this trial were enrolled from eastern Europe, where resource limitations and local practice standards might have affected the type of patient enrolled, or patient management. Patients from lower socioeconomic regions might have had increased disease burdens due to delays in both diagnosis and treatment, thus possibly worsening progression-free survival outcomes, especially in patients with ECOG performance status 1. Another hypothesis might be that eastern European patients who have access to clinical trials might have a higher socioeconomic status, thus leading to increased compliance and better than expected outcomes in patients with ECOG performance status 0. However it is worth noting that ECOG performance status 0 and 1 are broad categories that allow much clinical latitude and remain somewhat subjective. As improved clinical²² and molecular²³ prognostic categories are developed, older classifications (eg, ECOG performance status) are likely to be replaced with more objective ones in future clinical trials. Although caution should be exercised when comparing across trials, the AXIS⁸ trial appeared to include more patients with MSKCC poor risk than our trial; however, this difference might be explained by use of two different MSKCC risk criteria, based on line of therapy, in the present trial²⁰ versus the AXIS trial.²⁴ More patients in the present trial had axitinib dose increases, and fewer had axitinib dose reductions, compared with patients in the AXIS trial.⁸ This difference might be due to patients receiving first-line versus second-line therapy being able to better tolerate dose increases, and to differences between one of the axitinib dose-escalation criteria (in the AXIS trial, dose increases were not allowed in patients receiving antihypertensive therapy, whereas they were allowed here). This trial has shown that axitinib has clinical activity in the first-line treatment of metastatic renal-cell carcinoma (panel). At the cutoff date for our analysis, only 114 (40%) of 288 patients had died, and median overall survival had not been reached. We continue to follow patients for overall survival in the study, and plan to report the definitive results when they are mature and more interpretable. The safety profiles for axitinib and sorafenib in the present study were in line with adverse events reported in previously treated patients in AXIS, 8 and those reported with other VEGFR inhibitors in "**Panel: Research in context**\n\n**Systematic review**\nA systematic literature review was not done as part of the planning for this trial. However, clinical trial data had shown median progression-free survival of 13·7 months with axitinib in cytokine-refractory metastatic renal-cell carcinoma\u003Csup\u003E11,12\u003C/sup\u003E and median progression-free survival of 5·5 months\u003Csup\u003E6\u003C/sup\u003E and 5·7 months\u003Csup\u003E13\u003C/sup\u003E with sorafenib in cytokine-refractory and treatment-naive disease, respectively. On the basis of these results, we amended a randomised trial of second-line axitinib versus sorafenib in Asian patients with advanced renal-cell carcinoma to include a separate global treatment-naive population." **Interpretation** To our knowledge, this is the first phase 3 trial evaluating axitinib, a VEGF-receptor inhibitor, in patients with treatment-naive metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. Axitinib was found to have clinical activity, and a manageable safety profile in these patients. Median progression-free survival with axitinib in the overall population is similar to that reported in other first-line studies evaluating sunitinib⁵ or pazopanib⁷ for the treatment of metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. Given its clinical activity and differentiated toxicity profile, axitinib is a welcome addition to the treatment armamentarium for patients with metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. treatment-naive patients.^(5,7,13,21,25) Adverse event profiles differed between agents. Baseline mean FKSI-15 and FKSI-DRS scores in the present trial were similar to those observed in a general US population,26 and so improvement in quality of life was not expected. Maintenance of quality of life was regarded as a good outcome. A consistent finding in observed mean FKSI-15 and FKSI-DRS scores was shown here as in the AXIS trial,27 in which quality of life worsened at the end of treatment with both axitinib and sorafenib, presumably because disease progression worsened quality of life. In the longitudinal analysis, postbaseline scores for patient-reported outcomes were similar between axitinib and sorafenib over time, consistent with results from AXIS.27 Unlike the AXIS trial,8 although the time-to-deterioration composite endpoints of time to death, tumour progression, or meaningful worsening in quality of life appeared longer for axitinib versus sorafenib, the differences were not significant; thus for both patient groups clinical efficacy (progression-free survival) was achieved without sacrificing quality of life. This study had several limitations. The sample size was relatively small, designed to detect a large magnitude of difference. Sorafenib, the comparator in this trial, was chosen because of its activity and broad indication in advanced renal-cell carcinoma,¹⁶ as well as its availability in the regions where the trial was undertaken and experience with its use in the AXIS trial.⁸ In retrospect, this choice might have been a limitation of the present study in that axitinib was not compared with what are regarded as standard first-line drugs, sunitinib or pazopanib.²⁵ Additionally, patients and investigators were unmasked to treatment, which might have led to bias in safety and assessment of patient-reported outcomes; efficacy endpoints, however, were assessed by masked www.thelancet.com/oncology Vol 14 December 2013 1293 Articles independent review committee. Selection bias due to use of permuted blocks for generation of randomisation lists is another potential limitation; however, the effect is thought to be minimal since the investigators did not have access to the central randomisation list (used for all centres) and, hence, would not have been able to predict treatment assignment. To our knowledge, this is the first randomised phase 3 trial comparing axitinib with an active agent in the first-line setting. Although there was no significant difference between patient groups' progression-free survival, axitinib showed clinical activity and an acceptable safety profile as first-line therapy for patients with treatment-naive metastatic renal-cell carcinoma. Given that no therapy is curative for patients with this disease, continuing research is needed to identify new active drugs.
